title: Record: TTT LoRA + SOTA Training (10L MuonWD FP16Emb OvertoneInit)
body:
## Summary

Stacks LoRA test-time training evaluation onto the current SOTA training recipe. These two improvements are orthogonal and have not been combined in any prior submission:

- **Training**: 10L, Muon WD (0.02), FP16 tied embed, overtone spectral SVD init, phase-transition resid_mix, sliding window eval stride=64 (from `2026-03-19_SlidingWindow_FP16Emb_10L_MuonWD_OvertoneInit`, val_bpb=1.1748)
- **Evaluation**: Per-document LoRA adaptation (rank=8 on Q/V + LM head), document-isolated chunked evaluation with Adam optimizer (from `2026-03-17_LoRA_TTT`, val_bpb=1.1928 on baseline)

The TTT ablation showed ~0.037 bpb improvement from eval-time adaptations applied to the weaker baseline model. Applying the same technique to the SOTA-trained model is expected to yield ~1.14 val_bpb.

## Changes

- Added TTT hyperparameters to `Hyperparameters` class
- Modified `CausalSelfAttention.forward`, `Block.forward`, `GPT.forward` to accept optional LoRA delta parameters (no-op when not provided, preserving training behavior)
- Added `BatchedLinearLoRA`, `BatchedTTTLoRA` classes for batched per-document LoRA adaptation
- Added `eval_val_ttt_lora` function for document-isolated chunked LoRA evaluation
- After standard sliding window eval, runs TTT LoRA eval and reports both scores

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify training reproduces ~1.1748 val_bpb on standard sliding window eval (identical training path)
- [ ] Verify TTT LoRA eval produces improvement over standard eval
- [ ] Verify total eval time (sliding window + TTT) stays under 10 minutes
- [ ] Run 3 seeds for statistical significance (p < 0.01)
- [ ] Verify compressed artifact stays under 16MB

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

comments.body:
This file is 1537 lines long, which violates the script’s own stated hard limit of 1500 lines (docstring). Please shorten `train_gpt.py` (e.g., move TTT/sliding-eval helpers into a separate module file, or otherwise reduce code) so it stays within the 1500-line cap.
```suggestion

```

`eval_val_sliding` only adds windows while `p + seq_len <= total` and advances by `stride`. If `stride` does not evenly divide `(total - seq_len)`, the last `total % stride` tokens may never be scored. Consider either enforcing `seq_len % stride == 0`/`(total - seq_len) % stride == 0`, or explicitly adding a final window starting at `total - seq_len` to guarantee full coverage.
```suggestion

    # Ensure the tail of the sequence is covered even if (total - seq_len) is
    # not an exact multiple of stride.
    if windows:
        last_start, _ = windows[-1]
        last_scored = last_start + seq_len  # index of last scored target token
        if last_scored < total:
            final_start = total - seq_len
            # Choose offset so that the first scored target is last_scored + 1
            final_offset = last_scored - final_start
            if final_offset < 0:
                final_offset = 0
            elif final_offset >= seq_len:
                final_offset = seq_len - 1
            windows.append((final_start, final_offset))
```

`load_state_dict(..., strict=False)` will silently ignore missing/unexpected keys during the int8+zlib roundtrip, which can mask serialization/dequantization bugs and make the reported eval metrics unreliable. If there isn’t a known key mismatch to tolerate, switch this to `strict=True`; otherwise, capture and log the missing/unexpected key lists so failures are visible.
```suggestion
    base_model.load_state_dict(dequantize_state_dict_int8(quant_state), strict=True)
```
